title: The Solstice Fragment
description: Welcome to the DSTH Command Console. We've archived the latest telemetry packet from the Solstice-1 satellite, but our system is reporting minor decryption inconsistencies. As a third-party auditor, you must analyze the archived packet and verify the integrity of the data stream. Can you recover the secret flag embedded in the telemetry?
solution: The challenge implements a classic AES-CBC Padding Oracle vulnerability. 1) Access /api/telemetry/archive to get the IV and the target ciphertext. 2) Observe that /api/telemetry/verify returns distinct error codes: 'ERR_SIG_INVALID' when the PKCS#7 padding is incorrect (thrown by the unpad function) and 'ERR_HDR_MISMATCH' when padding is correct but the telemetry header 'AETHERIS-TLM:' is missing. 3) Use this side-channel to perform a bit-by-bit padding oracle attack. By manipulating the ciphertext blocks and sending them to the verify endpoint, an attacker can recover the plaintext block-by-block. 4) The decrypted plaintext will reveal the format 'AETHERIS-TLM:LEVELUP{...}:END'.

narrative_context.scenario:
Aetheris Orbital Dynamics recently deployed a constellation of low-earth orbit satellites designed for high-frequency trading and secure diplomatic communications. To manage the massive influx of sensitive data, they developed the Deep-Space Telemetry Hub (DSTH), which serves as the primary ground station interface. The hub is responsible for receiving encrypted telemetry packets from space assets, verifying their origin, and storing them in an immutable long-term archive for audit purposes.

While the system was built with standard cryptographic libraries, the internal security team is currently overwhelmed by the upcoming 'Solstice' launch window and hasn't had time to perform a rigorous review of the custom relay logic. Preliminary automated scans indicated that while the communication channels are encrypted, the way data blocks are processed for archival storage might not be as robust as originally intended. You have been brought in as a third-party security auditor to ensure that the encrypted data remains confidential even if an attacker gains access to the storage volumes.
narrative_context.app_purpose: Processes and archives encrypted sensor data and command logs from orbital satellites.
narrative_context.data_theme: Satellite telemetry packets, orbital coordinates, and encrypted command sequences.

flavor_details.recent_event: A series of anomalous bit-flips was detected in the Vesper-9 archival logs, leading to concerns about the block-level security of the stored data.

calibration_notes: Source analysis: The challenge implements a significantly hardened Padding Oracle. The attacker must first overcome a blinded oracle where responses are hashed and salted with a value that rotates every 8 requests, requiring constant re-synchronization of the 'Padding Error' fingerprint. Secondly, the padding scheme is non-standard; the padding length and verification bytes are determined by dynamic XOR masks indexed by values within the IV (specifically iv[15]), which breaks standard automated tools and require (target was 2.0)
